title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a college student called Josh talking about a field trip to Seabird Island. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a college student called Josh talking about a field trip to Seabird Island. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: A college student called Josh describes a field trip to Seabird Island.
extracts.audio_script: Hi, I’m Josh, and I’m going to describe the week I spent on Seabird Island last spring. Several students go there to collect information about wildlife, although the visit is also open to people studying tourism. I joined because observations made on the island counted towards my [9]geography course[/9]. I had considered biology, but it did not fit my timetable. We travelled early on a Monday. A small plane operates in summer, but at that time of year the only practical way to reach the island was by [10]ferry[/10]. Fortunately, the sea was calm, so nobody felt ill.||The island has one village and plenty of open land. Our accommodation was a former [11]lighthouse[/11] on the eastern coast, which had been modernised inside but still had its tall tower. From the top, we could see nesting areas without disturbing the birds. We were warned not to approach them closely. I had bought a camera before leaving, but the item I used much more often was a pair of [12]binoculars[/12], especially when birds were on rocks below us. The leaders checked that everybody could use them and record observations accurately.||Each morning we split into teams. A few people counted birds from the beach, but my group was asked to observe nests from the [13]cliff path[/13], a narrow route with excellent views but strong winds. We always went with a leader and stayed behind a safety barrier. The work was not only about birds. It was shocking to discover how much [14]plastic waste[/14] had washed onto the shore, so one afternoon we collected bags of it and noted where it was found. The results were later sent to an environmental group. We also took readings from the island’s small [15]weather station[/15], measuring rainfall and wind speed each evening.||Living there was simpler than being at home, but more comfortable than I had expected. I had assumed we would cook on camping stoves. Actually, the accommodation included a [16]shared kitchen[/16], and groups prepared evening meals in turn. We learned quickly that everyone wanted to eat after a long day outdoors. There was limited internet, which was useful when uploading data, though I was quite pleased not to spend much time on my phone. In the final two days, each group created a short [17]presentation[/17] explaining its findings, and mine focused on the rubbish collected near nesting sites.||There was also some time to enjoy the island. One optional activity was cycling around the village, but the bicycles were old and several people chose to rest. I signed up for a [18]boat trip[/18] around the northern cliffs, where we saw hundreds of birds on the water. The week made me far more confident about practical fieldwork, and I am now considering a longer conservation placement next year. If any of you are interested, I have brought information about the next college visit.

dialogue.speaker: josh
dialogue.text: Hi, I’m Josh, and I’m going to describe the week I spent on Seabird Island last spring. Several students go there to collect information about wildlife, although the visit is also open to people studying tourism. I joined because observations made on the island counted towards my geography course. I had considered biology, but it did not fit my timetable. We travelled early on a Monday. A small plane operates in summer, but at that time of year the only practical way to reach the island was by ferry. Fortunately, the sea was calm, so nobody felt ill.||The island has one village and plenty of open land. Our accommodation was a former lighthouse on the eastern coast, which had been modernised inside but still had its tall tower. From the top, we could see nesting areas without disturbing the birds. We were warned not to approach them closely. I had bought a camera before leaving, but the item I used much more often was a pair of binoculars, especially when birds were on rocks below us. The leaders checked that everybody could use them and record observations accurately.||Each morning we split into teams. A few people counted birds from the beach, but my group was asked to observe nests from the cliff path, a narrow route with excellent views but strong winds. We always went with a leader and stayed behind a safety barrier. The work was not only about birds. It was shocking to discover how much plastic waste had washed onto the shore, so one afternoon we collected bags of it and noted where it was found. The results were later sent to an environmental group. We also took readings from the island’s small weather station, measuring rainfall and wind speed each evening.||Living there was simpler than being at home, but more comfortable than I had expected. I had assumed we would cook on camping stoves. Actually, the accommodation included a shared kitchen, and groups prepared evening meals in turn. We learned quickly that everyone wanted to eat after a long day outdoors. There was limited internet, which was useful when uploading data, though I was quite pleased not to spend much time on my phone. In the final two days, each group created a short presentation explaining its findings, and mine focused on the rubbish collected near nesting sites.||There was also some time to enjoy the island. One optional activity was cycling around the village, but the bicycles were old and several people chose to rest. I signed up for a boat trip around the northern cliffs, where we saw hundreds of birds on the water. The week made me far more confident about practical fieldwork, and I am now considering a longer conservation placement next year. If any of you are interested, I have brought information about the next college visit.
